subject: social-studies
title: Grade 7 Social Studies — Texas History (Deep Dive)
description: Texas Grade 7 SS follows TEKS §113.19 — Texas History with analytical depth. Six core strands: Pre-Columbian to Spanish era, Mexican Texas to Revolution, Republic & Annexation, Civil War & Reconstruction, Industrial era (cattle/oil), Modern era & Texas economy. Authored 2026-05-17.

lessons.title: Indigenous Texas & Spanish Conquest
lessons.objective: Analyze Texas's earliest cultures and Spanish colonization.

questions.prompt: Which Spanish explorer was shipwrecked on the Texas coast in 1528 and spent 8 years living with and being enslaved by Native groups before returning to Mexico?
questions.answer: Álvar Núñez Cabeza de Vaca
questions.explanation: Cabeza de Vaca was shipwrecked near Galveston in 1528. He spent 8 years walking across what is now the southwestern United States and Mexico. His written account is the first European description of Texas.

questions.prompt: The Caddo Confederacy in East Texas had a complex society. Which of these is NOT true about the Caddo?
choices: They were skilled farmers who grew corn, beans, and squash
They built mounds and lived in permanent villages
They traded extensively with other Native groups
They were nomadic and rarely settled in one place
questions.answer: They were nomadic and rarely settled in one place
questions.explanation: The Caddo were the opposite of nomadic — they were SETTLED farmers who built permanent grass-roofed houses, ceremonial mounds, and complex trade networks. Their society had clear social classes and leaders called caddis.

questions.prompt: Why did Spain build missions and presidios in Texas in the 1700s?
choices: To extract gold and silver from Texas
To spread Catholicism, claim land, and defend against French expansion from Louisiana
To establish trade with the Comanche only
questions.answer: To spread Catholicism, claim land, and defend against French expansion from Louisiana
questions.explanation: Spain had three goals: spread Catholic Christianity through missions, claim and hold Texas territory, and block France (which controlled Louisiana). Missions were religious settlements; presidios were nearby military forts protecting them.

questions.prompt: Mission San Antonio de Valero, founded in 1718, is better known today by what name?
questions.answer: The Alamo
questions.explanation: Mission San Antonio de Valero was founded in 1718. Spanish soldiers later nicknamed it "el Alamo" (Spanish for "cottonwood," after a grove of trees nearby). It became the famous battle site in 1836.

questions.prompt: The Spanish mission system in Texas largely failed because:
choices: Diseases, conflicts with Native groups, and limited resources made missions hard to sustain
questions.answer: Diseases, conflicts with Native groups, and limited resources made missions hard to sustain
questions.explanation: European diseases killed many Native Americans the missions tried to convert. The Comanche and Apache resisted Spanish expansion. Spain didn't send enough soldiers, money, or settlers. Most Texas missions were closed by the early 1800s.

lessons.title: From Austin's Colony to San Jacinto
lessons.objective: Trace the causes and key events of the Texas Revolution.

questions.prompt: Stephen F. Austin's first colony of 300 American families had to agree to which Mexican requirements?
choices: Speak only Spanish at all times
Become Mexican citizens, convert to Catholicism, and obey Mexican laws
Pay Mexico 50% of all crops grown
questions.answer: Become Mexican citizens, convert to Catholicism, and obey Mexican laws
questions.explanation: Mexico's empresario system required settlers to become Mexican citizens, convert to Catholicism (officially), and follow Mexican law. In exchange, settlers got large amounts of cheap land. Many colonists honored these rules loosely, which created tension with Mexico.

questions.prompt: What single 1830 Mexican law CAUSED much of the tension that led to the Texas Revolution?
choices: The Law of April 6, 1830, which banned further immigration from the United States and increased taxes
A law requiring Texans to speak only French
A law dividing Texas into 100 small states
A law that gave all land back to the Comanche
questions.answer: The Law of April 6, 1830, which banned further immigration from the United States and increased taxes
questions.explanation: The Law of April 6, 1830 stopped legal U.S. immigration to Texas, put new soldiers in Texas, and raised customs duties. It made many Anglo-Texans feel Mexico had broken its bargain — a key cause of growing rebellion.

questions.prompt: The "Come and Take It" flag, raised at Gonzales in October 1835, marks the start of:
questions.answer: The Texas Revolution
questions.explanation: The Battle of Gonzales (October 2, 1835) was the FIRST armed clash of the Texas Revolution. Mexican soldiers came to take back a cannon Mexico had loaned the town; the townspeople raised a flag showing the cannon with the words "Come and Take It" — and won the small skirmish.

questions.prompt: Why did the Battle of the Alamo, despite being a Mexican military victory, ultimately help the Texas cause?
choices: It gave Sam Houston time to organize the Texas army and rally support
It convinced Mexico to give up immediately
questions.answer: It gave Sam Houston time to organize the Texas army and rally support
questions.explanation: The 13-day siege of the Alamo (Feb-March 1836) bought Sam Houston critical time to train soldiers, gather supplies, and rally support. The defenders' sacrifice also created the rallying cry "Remember the Alamo!" that motivated the Texan army to victory at San Jacinto six weeks later.

questions.prompt: The Battle of San Jacinto on April 21, 1836, lasted about how long?
questions.answer: Roughly 18 minutes
questions.explanation: The actual battle lasted only about 18 minutes (followed by hours of cleanup). Sam Houston's surprise attack during the Mexican army's afternoon rest period was so effective that Santa Anna's army of 1,400 was destroyed or captured. Santa Anna was caught the next day.

lessons.title: Texas as a Republic, then a State
lessons.objective: Analyze the challenges of the Republic of Texas and the path to U.S. statehood.

questions.prompt: Why did many Texans want the United States to annex (take in) Texas right after independence in 1836?
choices: Texas had massive debts and frequent fights with Mexico — joining the U.S. promised protection and stability
Texas wanted to become a French territory
The Republic of Texas had no flag
Texas was running out of land
questions.answer: Texas had massive debts and frequent fights with Mexico — joining the U.S. promised protection and stability
questions.explanation: The Republic of Texas was deeply in debt, threatened by Mexico (which never recognized Texas independence), and struggled to fund a working government. Annexation by the U.S. promised debt relief, military protection, and access to U.S. markets.

questions.prompt: U.S. annexation of Texas was DELAYED for nine years (1836-1845) mostly because:
choices: Adding Texas as a slave state would upset the balance with free states, and it risked war with Mexico
Mexico had already given Texas back to Spain
questions.answer: Adding Texas as a slave state would upset the balance with free states, and it risked war with Mexico
questions.explanation: Two big obstacles delayed Texas annexation: (1) Texas allowed slavery, so adding it would shift the balance in Congress between slave and free states; and (2) Mexico had warned that annexation would mean war. Both fears were realized in 1846 when the Mexican-American War began.

questions.prompt: Which war broke out almost immediately after Texas became a U.S. state in 1845?
questions.answer: The Mexican-American War
questions.explanation: The Mexican-American War (1846-1848) started over disputes about the U.S.-Mexico border after Texas joined the U.S. Mexico still considered Texas part of Mexico. The U.S. won, and the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo gave the U.S. huge new territory including California, Arizona, New Mexico, and more.

questions.prompt: Mirabeau Lamar, the second president of the Republic of Texas, is most associated with:
choices: Making Spanish the only language of Texas
Founding a public education system and making Austin the capital
questions.answer: Founding a public education system and making Austin the capital
questions.explanation: Lamar (president 1838-1841) set aside land for public schools, which is why he's sometimes called the "Father of Texas Education." He also moved the capital to the small town of Waterloo and renamed it Austin. His policies toward Native Americans were harsh and led to the forced removal of the Cherokee from East Texas.

questions.prompt: Sam Houston's relationship with the U.S. government before he became Texas president included:
choices: He served as governor of Tennessee and as a U.S. Congressman before coming to Texas
He never met any U.S. presidents
questions.answer: He served as governor of Tennessee and as a U.S. Congressman before coming to Texas
questions.explanation: Sam Houston had a remarkable career: U.S. Congressman from Tennessee, governor of Tennessee, then moved to Texas, became the hero of San Jacinto, twice president of the Republic of Texas, U.S. Senator from Texas, AND governor of Texas. Few Americans served in more leadership roles.

units.title: Texas in the Civil War & Reconstruction

lessons.title: Secession, Civil War & Reconstruction in Texas
lessons.objective: Analyze Texas's role in the Civil War and the Reconstruction era.

questions.prompt: Texas voted to secede from the United States in February 1861. The main reason was:
choices: Disagreement over the protection and expansion of slavery, plus broader states'-rights conflicts
Britain offered Texas a better deal
The Texas legislature wanted to rejoin Mexico
questions.answer: Disagreement over the protection and expansion of slavery, plus broader states'-rights conflicts
questions.explanation: Texas's official Declaration of Causes of Secession (Feb 1861) explicitly cited the protection of slavery and disagreements with the federal government's stance on slavery as the main reasons. States' rights conflicts and economic disagreements also played a role.

questions.prompt: Sam Houston, who was governor of Texas in 1861, took what action when Texas voted to secede?
choices: He celebrated and led the Confederate Texas army
He refused to take the oath of loyalty to the Confederacy and was removed from office
He immediately joined the Union army
questions.answer: He refused to take the oath of loyalty to the Confederacy and was removed from office
questions.explanation: Sam Houston opposed secession — he believed it would destroy Texas. When the Texas legislature required all officials to swear loyalty to the Confederacy, Houston refused. He was removed from the governorship and died in Huntsville in 1863 before the war ended.

questions.prompt: Most Civil War battles fought ON Texas soil were:
choices: Massive battles with tens of thousands of soldiers
Relatively small clashes, since most of the war was fought east of the Mississippi
questions.answer: Relatively small clashes, since most of the war was fought east of the Mississippi
questions.explanation: Most major Civil War battles happened in Virginia, Tennessee, Mississippi, and Georgia. Texas saw smaller engagements (like the Battle of Galveston). Interestingly, the last battle of the Civil War — Palmito Ranch in May 1865 — was fought in Texas, more than a month AFTER the official surrender.

questions.prompt: Why did the news that enslaved people were free not reach Texas until June 19, 1865 — over two years after the Emancipation Proclamation?
choices: Texas was the most western Confederate state, federal troops did not arrive to enforce the order until June 1865
Texas had no enslaved people before that date
questions.answer: Texas was the most western Confederate state, federal troops did not arrive to enforce the order until June 1865
questions.explanation: The Emancipation Proclamation only freed enslaved people in CONFEDERATE-held areas, and only when Union troops arrived to enforce it. Texas was the westernmost Confederate state with the fewest Union troops. General Granger arrived in Galveston June 19, 1865 — that's the day we commemorate as Juneteenth.

questions.prompt: During Reconstruction (1865-1877), what major change to Texas law and government occurred?
choices: Texas became part of Mexico again
A new state constitution was written that, among other things, allowed Black men to vote and serve in office
All Texans were required to move out of the state
Texas split into four smaller states
questions.answer: A new state constitution was written that, among other things, allowed Black men to vote and serve in office
questions.explanation: Reconstruction-era constitutions (the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments to the U.S. Constitution and Texas's 1869 constitution) extended voting rights to Black men and required equal protection under law. Many Black Texans served in the legislature during this period. After Reconstruction ended in 1877, many of these gains were rolled back by Jim Crow laws.

lessons.title: Cattle Kingdom, Oil Boom & Railroads
lessons.objective: Analyze the economic transformations of Texas from 1870 to 1930.

questions.prompt: The era of the great Texas cattle drives (roughly 1866-1885) ended because:
choices: Railroads reached deep into Texas, making long drives unnecessary, and barbed wire fenced off the open range
questions.answer: Railroads reached deep into Texas, making long drives unnecessary, and barbed wire fenced off the open range
questions.explanation: Two innovations killed the cattle-drive era: railroads expanded southward (so ranchers shipped cattle by train instead of driving them north to Kansas), and barbed wire (invented 1874) let landowners fence off pastures, ending the open range.

questions.prompt: The Spindletop oil discovery (January 10, 1901) is significant because:
choices: It produced more oil in a day than every other well in the world combined and started the modern oil industry
It was the first time anyone in Texas drilled for oil
It produced gold instead of oil
questions.answer: It produced more oil in a day than every other well in the world combined and started the modern oil industry
questions.explanation: Spindletop blew oil 150 feet into the air at 100,000 barrels per day — more than every other oil well in the world combined at the time. It transformed Texas into a major oil-producing region and made oil a cheap, abundant energy source for the 20th century.

questions.prompt: Which industries grew rapidly in Texas because of cheap oil and easy transportation in the early 1900s?
choices: Refining, petrochemicals, automobile manufacturing, and shipping
questions.answer: Refining, petrochemicals, automobile manufacturing, and shipping
questions.explanation: Cheap Texas oil powered new industries: oil refineries turned crude into gasoline; petrochemical plants made plastics and fertilizers; shipping ports grew at Houston and Galveston; and the affordable gasoline supported the rise of the automobile industry nationally.

questions.prompt: Which of these MOST affected Texas agriculture in the early 1900s?
choices: The boll weevil destroyed cotton crops, leading to the rise of new crops and mechanization
questions.answer: The boll weevil destroyed cotton crops, leading to the rise of new crops and mechanization
questions.explanation: The boll weevil, a beetle that arrived in Texas around 1892, devastated cotton crops. Farmers diversified — turning to citrus in the Rio Grande Valley, vegetables, and ranching — and mechanized farming to fight pests and labor shortages.

questions.prompt: The Great Depression (1929-1939) hit Texas especially hard because:
choices: Oil prices collapsed AND a major drought called the Dust Bowl ruined farmland across the Great Plains
Texas was the only state affected
questions.answer: Oil prices collapsed AND a major drought called the Dust Bowl ruined farmland across the Great Plains
questions.explanation: Texas faced a double crisis: oil prices crashed (because too many new fields had been discovered), AND severe drought turned parts of the Texas Panhandle into the Dust Bowl. Many farmers lost everything and moved west. New Deal programs eventually brought some relief.

lessons.title: Texas in the Late 20th & 21st Century
lessons.objective: Analyze the modern Texas economy and demographic shifts.

questions.prompt: Which Texas city became the headquarters of the U.S. space program in the 1960s?
questions.answer: Houston
questions.explanation: NASA's Manned Spacecraft Center (renamed Johnson Space Center in 1973) opened near Houston in 1963. Houston was Mission Control for Apollo, the Space Shuttle, and continues to train astronauts today. "Houston, we have a problem" became one of the most famous lines in space history.

questions.prompt: Today, what is the LARGEST sector of the Texas economy?
choices: Energy + petrochemicals are huge, but services (finance, healthcare, technology) actually employ more Texans than any other sector
questions.answer: Energy + petrochemicals are huge, but services (finance, healthcare, technology) actually employ more Texans than any other sector
questions.explanation: Texas is famous for oil and gas, which generate huge revenue. But the service sector — including finance, healthcare, retail, and technology — employs the most Texans. Austin and Dallas have become major tech hubs, sometimes called "Silicon Hills."

questions.prompt: Texas's population has shifted significantly. Today, Texas is:
choices: More than 85% urban — most Texans live in cities and suburbs
The smallest U.S. state by population
Larger than the rest of the United States combined
questions.answer: More than 85% urban — most Texans live in cities and suburbs
questions.explanation: Despite Texas's wide-open image, over 85% of Texans live in metropolitan areas. The five largest cities — Houston, San Antonio, Dallas, Austin, and Fort Worth — all rank among the 20 most populous U.S. cities.

questions.prompt: Which group is the FASTEST-growing portion of the Texas population in the 21st century?
questions.answer: Hispanic/Latino Texans
questions.explanation: The Hispanic/Latino population — which has long historical roots in Texas — has been the fastest-growing major group for decades. The 2020 U.S. Census found Hispanic/Latino Texans make up about 40% of Texas's population, on track to soon be the largest single group.

questions.prompt: Texas has no state income tax. What is the LARGEST source of state revenue today?
choices: Sales taxes (especially on retail purchases) plus oil and gas production taxes
Tourism taxes from out-of-state visitors only
questions.answer: Sales taxes (especially on retail purchases) plus oil and gas production taxes
questions.explanation: Texas pays for state services mainly through sales tax, business taxes, and taxes on oil and gas production. Property taxes (local) fund schools. Texas is one of only nine U.S. states with no state income tax — a deliberate policy choice.
